mentions.context: Harbor Fog Captain Elias Thorn stood on the deck of the Heron and waited for the fog to lift. Mira Vale walked to the rail and looked at the gray water. The lighthouse of Port Saffron blinked in the distance.
The lighthouse of Port Saffron blinked in the distance. "There," Mira Vale said. Elias Thorn nodded.
"They will bleed us dry," Elias Thorn said. Mira Vale smiled and rolled up her charts without answering.

The Granite Hall The Salt Guild kept its offices in a granite hall above the fish market of Port Saffron. Mira Vale waited on a bench while the clerks argued behind tall doors. Captain Elias Thorn came out an hour later with empty hands.

"Confiscated," Elias Thorn said. "Every crate." Mira Vale looked at the granite hall for a long moment. Then Mira Vale whispered the beginning of a plan, and Elias Thorn laughed for the first time in days.
"Every crate." Mira Vale looked at the granite hall for a long moment. Then Mira Vale whispered the beginning of a plan, and Elias Thorn laughed for the first time in days.
Warehouse Nine That night Mira Vale found the junior clerk drinking alone in the café. Mira Vale asked careful questions and bought careful answers.

Warehouse Nine That night Mira Vale found the junior clerk drinking alone in the café. Mira Vale asked careful questions and bought careful answers. Warehouse nine held the cargo of the Heron, the clerk admitted at last.

Warehouse nine held the cargo of the Heron, the clerk admitted at last. Mira Vale smiled, paid the keeper, and slipped back into the dark. By the old wall on the 9th pier, Captain Elias Thorn waited with two lanterns and a crowbar.

North The Heron slipped her moorings before the dawn bells rang. Captain Elias Thorn held the wheel while Mira Vale watched the lights shrink behind them, the recovered cargo lashed tight below. The Salt Guild would write its angry letters to every port on the coast—harbor masters, tax men & brokers alike.

The Salt Guild would write its angry letters to every port on the coast—harbor masters, tax men & brokers alike. Mira Vale looked north and said nothing. Elias Thorn smiled at the open water and set the course past the shoals.
Mira Vale walked to the rail and looked at the gray water. The lighthouse of Port Saffron blinked in the distance. "There," Mira Vale said.
The Granite Hall The Salt Guild kept its offices in a granite hall above the fish market of Port Saffron. Mira Vale waited on a bench while the clerks argued behind tall doors.
Elias Thorn smiled at the open water and set the course past the shoals. Port Saffron sank back into the fog it had come from.
Elias Thorn nodded. The Salt Guild controlled every pier along that shore, and its fees had doubled since spring. "They will bleed us dry," Elias Thorn said.

Warehouse Nine That night Mira Vale found the junior clerk drinking alone in the café. Mira Vale asked careful questions and bought careful answers.

Mira Vale asked careful questions and bought careful answers. Warehouse nine held the cargo of the Heron, the clerk admitted at last. Mira Vale smiled, paid the keeper, and slipped back into the dark.
By the old wall on the 9th pier, Captain Elias Thorn waited with two lanterns and a crowbar. "Warehouse nine," Mira Vale whispered. Elias Thorn nodded once and picked up the iron bar.
